⚠ Disclaimer & Data Attribution

This report was prepared by Narissara Khomkhai as a candidate portfolio demonstration for the Associate Vice President, University Development & Advancement Solutions role at Harvard University. It is designed to illustrate executive reporting architecture, AI-enabled operations, and leadership decision support for advancement strategy.

All data presented is sourced directly from Harvard Published Data – Harvard FAS Annual Reports, HKS Annual Reports, Harvard Management Company reports, and publicly available campaign figures. I do not claim ownership of this data, and it is used here for illustrative data-presenting purposes only.

Where official published figures were not available at the fund level, illustrative sample data has been used – clearly marked throughout the report – to demonstrate reporting architecture, dashboard design, and analytical methodology.

This report should not be cited as an official source of information. It is not affiliated with, endorsed by, or produced by Harvard University or Harvard Kennedy School.

Copyright notice: © 2026 Narissara Khomkhai. All rights reserved. This strategic blueprint, report design, data architecture, workflows, and illustrative analysis are provided only for portfolio review and may not be copied, redistributed, reused, or adapted without written permission.

0.0 · Signature Idea · Advancement Signal Tower

Driving Harvard’s Fundraising Future

The proposal is not that Harvard needs to start over. It is that AI is changing the speed of work faster than most organizations can comfortably absorb. In technology, that shift can feel like a quiet black horse moving just behind the field: suddenly close, suddenly fast. This model helps buy time back for leaders, schools, and gift officers by turning scattered signals into clearer direction.

01 · Control View

VP / leadership gets the control view.

Portfolio health, school alignment, campaign risk, and advancement momentum become visible in one decision environment.

02 · Keep the Train Reliable

UAAD becomes the operating spine.

Data, workflow, dashboards, report design, and governance keep every car connected without forcing schools into one-size-fits-all execution.

03 · Move with Signals

Schools and gift officers receive clearer direction.

New Track Alerts and Whisper Box inputs help leadership detect emerging opportunities, risks, and blockers before they become late-stage surprises.

Metaphor: a well-instructed train can keep moving when the rails, signals, conductor, and destination are clear. The goal is to reduce constant manual pushing, preserve school nuance, and help teams move earlier with better information.
The idea

Build an advancement signal layer that helps excellent teams move with less friction.

Harvard already has deep expertise, donor relationships, and school knowledge. The opportunity is to connect those strengths into a shared operating picture so the next trend, risk, or opportunity is easier to see.

Why now

AI is no longer a distant topic; it is a quiet acceleration behind daily work.

The aim is not to replace human judgment. It is to give people more time, cleaner context, and practical support before complexity turns into delay.

What UAAD enables

A reliable operating spine for coordinated fundraising execution.

New Track Alerts, Whisper Box inputs, data governance, dashboards, report design, and school plug-ins become one leadership-ready system: proceed, hold, align, escalate, or reroute.

Positioning: This is a time-saving and trend-sensing proposal, not a critique of Harvard’s current strengths. It shows how AI-enabled intelligence can make already talented people’s work easier, faster to coordinate, and more visible to leadership.
Section 1.1 · Proposal · AI-Driven Transformation

Strategic Opportunity: Harvard's Advancement Advantage

Harvard's advancement function has built extraordinary momentum — $1.42B raised in FY26, 81% AI adoption across schools, a $2.1B nine-figure pipeline, and 20-year relationships with the world's most transformational donors. The opportunity ahead is to build the intelligence infrastructure that turns this momentum into a generational achievement: a $10B+ campaign that reflects the full depth of Harvard's relationship capital, coordinated with a precision that no peer institution can match.

The Transformation Thesis

Harvard is poised to evolve its already premier advancement operation into a future-ready powerhouse. By integrating AI automation, a federated data spine, and a structured four-level operating model, we are empowering our 12 schools and central offices to harmonize like never before. This transformation unites our alumni programs and giving infrastructure into a single, intelligent ecosystem—marking a historic new chapter of coordinated excellence.

The Four-Level Advancement Opportunity

01
Central Level
University Development Office (UDO)
Principal Gifts · Presidential Priorities · University-Wide Coordination
$2.1B
9-Figure Pipeline
Opportunity

Build a Central Donor Master ID across all 12 schools so every presidential gift conversation is informed by Harvard's full relationship depth — not just one school's view.

AI Capability

Presidential Priority Alignment Engine scores all 500+ managed prospects against 6 Garber-era priorities weekly. Briefing Pack Generator delivers comprehensive meeting intelligence in 12 minutes, not 3 hours.

Impact Potential

Eliminate 23 cross-school cultivation conflicts per quarter. Achieve 95% presidential priority alignment. Unlock $200M+ in new international pipeline from Gulf, India, and Southeast Asia.

Central Donor Master: In Progress Priority Engine: Pre-Build Briefing Pack: Prototype Live FY26 Investment: $2.2M
02
School-Based Level
12 Schools · FAS, HBS, HMS, HKS, HLS, SEAS and More
School-Specific Dean's Priorities · Annual Funds · School Gift Officers
9/12
Schools CRM-Live
Opportunity

Each of 12 schools has a unique donor community, dean's priority, and annual fund model. AI enables each school to run school-specific propensity variants and briefing tools while contributing to Harvard's shared Central Master — preserving school autonomy and enabling university coordination.

AI Capability

School-specific XGBoost propensity models (one per school). HKS example: $124.6M raised FY25, 6.7:1 ROI, 35:1 planned giving ROI. CRM Analytics Upgrade unlocks AI portfolio scoring for all 8,642 active HKS donors at weekly cadence.

Impact Potential

$2.8B cross-school pipeline coordinated without duplication. Gift velocity +30% for GSD, HGSE, HLS on CRM migration. HKS planned giving pipeline from 62% to 80% coverage (35:1 ROI). 20/20 school federation required for FY28 campaign launch.

03
Constituency Level
Harvard College Fund (HCF)
Alumni Class Giving · Reunion-Based Fundraising · Loyalty Society · Leave a Legacy
400K+
Total Alumni Pool
Opportunity

HCF is the cultivation engine for every future major gift at every school. An alumnus who gives consistently for 8+ years is worth 6× more in lifetime value than one who lapses at year 3. AI propensity scoring activates the 124 high-capacity alumni currently waiting for outreach that manual workflows cannot deliver.

AI Capability

RFM-based giving propensity model with weekly refresh. Loyalty streak alert system (45-day silence trigger). Bequest likelihood model for Leave a Legacy (35:1 ROI, highest in HCF portfolio). Matching gift automation: 82%→95% capture. Digital platform AI personalisation.

Impact Potential

Donor retention 74.8%→80%. 500+ Leave a Legacy commitments by FY27. Matching gift uplift +$1.5M/yr. Dean's Council 90 new members/yr. Class participation 42.8%→50% in graduating class campaign. Each retention gain feeds major gift pipelines across all 12 schools.

RFM Model: Design Phase Bequest Model: Pre-Build FY26 Investment: $1.17M Matching Gift: In Progress
04
Engagement Level
Harvard Alumni Association (HAA)
Global Alumni Clubs · Shared Interest Groups · Events · Volunteer Pathways
84
Countries · 180+ Clubs
Opportunity

HAA manages Harvard's broadest alumni relationship — 400,000+ alumni across 84 countries through 180+ clubs and 200+ shared interest groups. An alumnus who participates in 3+ HAA events per year and volunteers in a leadership role is 4× more likely to make a leadership gift. AI makes this pathway systematic at scale.

AI Capability

Global Engagement Score (0–100) for all 400K+ alumni with weekly refresh. Shared Interest Group Signal Router: NLP classification of group participation against school priorities, automatically routing to relevant development offices. Development Handoff API: automated 48-hour alert to school gift officer when engagement threshold is crossed.

Impact Potential

500+ high-value alumni handoffs to school development offices per year. +30% event-to-volunteer conversion. 100% of 400K+ alumni engagement-scored by FY27. 250+ campaign volunteer candidates identified for FY2028 campaign council. 42% of Harvard's donors are international — HAA's global intelligence feeds the UDO's international pipeline.

target by FY27

Four-Level AI Transformation · Combined Investment & Impact Summary

From fragmented activity to one executive performance system.

AI becomes the intelligence layer across central UDO, schools, community fundraising, and alumni engagement—so Harvard leaders can monitor adoption, pipeline movement, data health, and team effectiveness in one place.

Total FY26 build $6.62M Modeled investment
01 · Central UDO$2.20M

Principal gift intelligence, campaign command view, and executive-risk triage.

School-specific intake, data quality workflow, and dean-ready prospect briefs.

03 · Community Giving$1.17M

Fund segmentation, stewardship triggers, and recurring donor renewal signals.

Club, SIG, and global engagement bridge for volunteer-driven pipeline growth.

Section I · Executive Strategic Proposal · Part of the UD&AS Strategic Intelligence Series

AI-Driven Organizational Transformation Masterplan

A full proposal for resolving school-vs-central data conflict through one operating spine: School Data → Central Master ID → Gift Admin Ops. This report is part of the AI Transition & Strategic Intelligence Console — which connects this operational blueprint to Harvard’s long-term demographic and economic strategy through FY2075.

Visual Hub · Matrix-First Operating Model
School context becomes central truth, then Gift Admin action.

Designed for senior review: each section uses action-first bullets, short scan lines, and explicit outcomes tied to velocity, accuracy, and liquidity.

Velocity1.8 Days
Accuracy90%
Liquidity ID$1.4B
Input
School Data

Relationship notes, faculty ties, proposal stage, dean priorities, and school-specific donor history.

->
Resolver
Central Master ID

One identity spine reconciles households, aliases, ownership, restrictions, intent, and timing.

->
Action
Gift Admin Ops

Automated intent classification routes gifts, exceptions, stewardship, and liquidity signals.

School Need

Preserve: relationship nuance, officer ownership, dean strategy, and faculty context.

Central Need

Standardize: identity, restrictions, approvals, gift coding, and leadership reporting.

Matrix Answer

Connect: schools keep context; central owns truth rules; Gift Admin executes one trusted queue.

I
Infrastructure
Outcome: trusted data spine
  • Unify: Resolve school records into one Master ID without erasing local ownership.
  • Govern: Define shared rules for aliases, households, restrictions, and approvals.
  • Expose: Show owner, data source, and confidence score in each advancement view.
II
Stewardship Velocity
Outcome: 1.8-day gift cycle
  • Classify: Use AI-driven Automated Intent Classification at gift intake.
  • Route: Move exceptions to specialists and standard gifts to straight-through processing.
  • Close: Trigger audit-ready coding, acknowledgment, and stewardship follow-up.
III
Research Precision
Outcome: 90% signal confidence
  • Score: Apply NLP Affinity Scoring to notes, event signals, and proposal language.
  • Prioritize: Rank prospects by capacity, timing, theme fit, and readiness.
  • Recommend: Surface the right school partner, officer, case theme, and next move.
IV
Campaign Roadmap
Outcome: $1.4B liquidity map
  • Sequence: Move from Master ID pilot to school adoption to campaign-wide use.
  • Measure: Track velocity, accuracy, liquidity, duplication, and cross-school gifts.
  • Scale: Convert liquidity intelligence into flexible, near-term campaign capacity.

Full proposal continues below. Snapshot KPIs and charts remain visible; accordions expand for implementation detail.

Why This Portfolio Matters Now

UD&AS is the analytical, operational, and technological backbone for Harvard's $50B+ endowment philanthropy ecosystem. FY2026 operates against federal funding constraints, an active PPF drive, and the infrastructure work required to launch Harvard's next comprehensive campaign by FY2028.

$53.2BEndowment FY2024
$9.6BHarvard Campaign 2013–2018: the benchmark
$10B+Next campaign target, FY2028 launch
$9.6B
2013–2018 Campaign

Record-setting. Set the model for cross-school coordination we now modernise.

$1.42B
FY2026 Annual

Foundation year. Infrastructure completes. Feasibility study commissioned Q3.

$10B+
FY2028 Campaign

AI endowment. Diversity chairs. Financial aid at scale. UD&AS is the backbone.

Campaign Architecture

Quiet phase FY2027: 20 solicitations at $25M+. Public launch FY2028 with $1.5B already committed. The infrastructure we build today is the foundation the next campaign stands on.

UD&AS unifies prospect research, predictive analytics, gift administration, and CRM modernisation into one coordinated operating model. Twelve schools keep their autonomy; the President gets one view.

Prospect ResearchAI propensity scoring, wealth ensemble, and liquidity event monitoring surface the right donor at the right moment.
Gift AdministrationGAAP-compliant 1.8-day cycle with automated intent classification and same-day 7-figure acknowledgment.
Advancement SolutionsCRM infrastructure, data governance, and IDEAS Portal keep all 12 schools coordinated without losing their identity.

The milestone portfolio contains seven workstreams: five are active in FY2026 and two were completed in FY2025. Status is shown as a simple indicator, not a progress bar, because these are governance gates rather than percentage-complete tasks.

Section 2.0 · Organizational Structure

Harvard Development Organizational Architecture

UD&AS sits at the center of a federated advancement model — coordinating 12 semi-autonomous school offices without holding formal authority over any of them. This is both the structural challenge and the strategic opportunity.

Core Operating Principle
"Zero Formal Authority" — Maximum Strategic Influence

UD&AS does not command school development offices — it earns their reliance by delivering intelligence, automation, and coordination that each school cannot replicate alone. Every AI initiative enhances the human touch of gift officers rather than replacing it; by absorbing repetitive administrative tasks, we free frontline staff to focus on the high-impact strategy and donor relationship-building that only people can do.

Zero
Formal Authority
100%
Service Value
12
School Partners
2.4×
Gift Size Lift
Organizational Hierarchy

Harvard University Development & Advancement Solutions

COFD Protocol

Collaborative Outreach & Facilitated Decision

Because UD&AS holds zero formal authority over Deans, prospect conflicts between schools are resolved through the COFD protocol — a structured consensus framework that replaces hierarchy with process. The protocol converts competing interests into shared wins, ensuring no Dean feels overruled and no donor receives duplicate solicitations.

STEP 1
Flag: UD&AS Research identifies a multi-school prospect and raises a coordination request via the Dean's Consensus Tracker.
STEP 2
Share: All relevant gift officers and Deans receive a joint prospect brief with the full relationship history visible to all parties.
STEP 3
Agree: Schools reach consensus on lead school, ask split, timeline, and relationship ownership within a 5-business-day window.
STEP 4
Escalate: If consensus is not reached, VP Development facilitates a structured decision. UD&AS never adjudicates — it mediates.
Human-Touch Strategy

AI Augments — It Does Not Replace

Every automation initiative is evaluated against a single question: does this free gift officers to do more of what only humans can do? The strategic intent is to compress administrative burden so that advancement professionals spend more time in authentic donor relationships — not less.

40%
Admin time reclaimed
+15%
Donor retention target
3×
More donor face time
-40%
Grant prep time target

The Human Touch Principle

Automation handles the predictable; people handle the irreplaceable. Data entry, prospect scoring, thank-you drafts, and meeting notes are machine territory. Strategy, empathy, and relationship judgment belong to your gift officers.

Section II · Strategy Frameworks

"One Harvard" Six Strategic Pillars

Six frameworks bridging relationship management with data infrastructure. Each pillar is expandable; open to read methodology, data, and case examples.

214
Active prospects
$2.8B
Total pipeline value
71%
Solicitation conversion

Harvard has 400K+ alumni. It is impossible to manually evaluate who might be ready to give a major gift. Our gradient boosting model scores every constituent weekly on 7 signals: recency, engagement velocity, wealth screening, event attendance, email behaviour, liquidity events, and peer network giving.

Step 1
Identify
AI flags wealth ≥9, engagement ≥75
Step 2
Qualify
Officer discovery + capacity validation
Step 3
Cultivate
President / Dean touchpoints, 2–4 meetings
Step 4
Solicit
Stage-gate review · VP Husson sign-off
Step 5
Steward
Impact reports · re-cultivation for next gift
Pipeline operating view: Weekly ranked prospect lists help managers see where cultivation, feasibility, and solicitation work is moving, and where leadership attention can unlock the next stage.
Pipeline Stage
enlarge ↗
9/12
Schools live
2.4×
Avg coordinated gift
$155M
Cross-school FY26 YTD

Harvard's 12 schools maintain independent gift officers, donor data, and stewardship calendars. Without a federated layer, the same $50M prospect may be approached by HBS, HMS, and HKS within 30 days. The Central Donor Master synchronises identity, capacity, and engagement state without overriding school autonomy.

Federal Research Trend
enlarge ↗

Closed Cross-School Gifts FY24–FY26 (Illustrative)

HMS+SEAS AI Initiative – $85M · HKS+GSD+FAS Climate – $42M · FAS+HGSE+HLS Access & Equity – $28M

A simulated Campaign Command Centre tracking real-time progress toward a $10B+ comprehensive campaign – measured against the Harvard Campaign benchmark (FY13–FY18: $9.6B). Three readiness scenarios modelled: $7B conservative, $10B base, $12B+ ambitious.

Readiness Scenarios vs. Pipeline Coverage ($B)
click to expand ↗

Declared donor interests are linked to Garber-era initiatives using 3 years of engagement data. Theme-matched asks close at 1.8× the rate of generic solicitations. The mapping informs both gift officer conversation strategy and the President's cultivation talking points.

01
Listen for Signals
Event attendance, faculty engagement, volunteer activity, and past giving identify the donor's strongest two or three themes.
02
Match to Priority
Themes are mapped to presidential priorities, school readiness, gift capacity, and timing so officers know what case belongs in the conversation.
03
Route the Ask
The right school, gift officer, dean, or President enters only when the donor theme, relationship owner, and proposal package are aligned.
AI / Technology – 88%Match → HMS AI Institute
Financial Aid – 95%Match → FAS/HBS scholarships
Climate – 74%Match → GSD Sustainable Lab
Civil Discourse – 65%Match → HKS/FAS chairs
Public Health – 80%Match → HMS Global Health
Theme-Matched ROI – 1.8×Close rate vs. generic ask

Pipeline Prospect Map

Open theme-matched donors, likely gift size, gift officer, and ZIP-based assignment logic.

Top Prospect Queue

Open the leadership call list behind the theme map and presidential-priority matching.

Tracks $184M+ in Green Gifts earmarked for the 2026 fossil-fuel-neutral milestone. Sustainability gifts also serve as a gateway for donors who lack a strong school affiliation – broadening Harvard's philanthropic base beyond traditional alumni.

Sustainability scorecard metrics by fiscal year
click to expand ↗
MetricFY24FY25FY26 YTD2026 TargetStatus
Green Gifts ($M)$118M$151M$184M$250M74% ▲
Endowed Sustainability Chairs3581267%
Salata Institute Funding ($M)$42M$58M$71M$90M79%
Donors flagged "climate" interest2,1402,6102,9803,50085%

The Flexibility Deficit – Harvard's Most Under-Addressed Funding Risk

Fixed endowments cannot be redeployed for immediate research crises. When NIH grants are interrupted, a $10M endowed professorship cannot bridge the gap. UD&AS scores constituents on flexibility propensity – historical pattern of giving to general funds vs. named restrictions – and prioritises these 380 donors for Presidential Priorities Fund cultivation.

Gap Analysis – Restricted Endowment vs. Operating Need ($B)
click to expand ↗

Combined geographic wealth signals (DonorSearch + iWave ensemble), alumni engagement scores, and historical giving trajectories surfaced a hidden cluster of high-capacity unmanaged prospects. 45 "unmanaged" prospects with capacity ≥$10M identified but unassigned to a gift officer for ≥18 months.

Synthetic prospect-density grid · darker crimson = higher concentration of $10M+ unmanaged prospects.

The Result

A coordinated trip for President Garber and three School Deans (HBS, SEAS, HMS) resulted in $25M in new verbal commitments across 7 prospect meetings – a 4.3× ROI on staff time vs. prior unaffiliated outreach.

Section 2.1a · AI Adoption Pillars

Strategic Pillars for AI Adoption

Three non-negotiable foundations that every AI initiative must satisfy before deployment. These pillars are the organizational pre-conditions for responsible, measurable, and durable AI transformation.

The Three Non-Negotiables

Before any AI tool is deployed, it must pass through three gates: mission, data, and ethics.

AI without measurable mission alignment is a cost center. AI without clean data produces confident errors. AI without ethical guardrails creates legal risk, donor distrust, and institutional reputation damage. All three pillars must be in place — and periodically audited — for AI to deliver compounding returns to Harvard's advancement mission.

Pillar I
Mission Alignment
Every AI initiative must serve a measurable goal

An AI tool that does not connect to a specific, quantified advancement objective should not be deployed. Before any initiative begins, the team must define a concrete mission target it serves.

Target Example 1
+15% donor retention
From 74.8% → 86% by FY27 via AI-powered lapse prediction and personalized re-engagement workflows
Target Example 2
-40% grant application time
From 18 hours → 11 hours per application via AI-assisted drafting, auto-population, and research synthesis
Target Example 3
1.8-day gift processing cycle
From 4.6 days (FY24) → 1.8 days via automated intent classification and straight-through processing

Governance rule: every AI initiative requires a mission owner, a baseline metric, and a 90-day checkpoint. No baseline, no deployment.

Pillar II
Data Foundation
AI is only as good as the data it processes

Before deploying any predictive or generative model, the underlying data must meet a minimum quality standard. A propensity model trained on incomplete records produces confident mispredictions that are worse than no model at all.

Data Readiness Checklist
✓Clean donor lists: deduplicated, accurate contact data, verified giving history for all active prospects
✓2+ years of financial history: minimum two complete fiscal years of transaction data for each constituent used in modeling
⟳Central Master ID: single identity spine reconciling households, aliases, and school-level records (9/12 live, 3 migrating)
✓Engagement signals: event attendance, email behavior, faculty ties, proposal stage in unified CRM layer
⟳Nightly wealth refresh: DonorSearch and iWave API integration for real-time capacity updates (HLS, GSD, HGSE pending)
9/12
Schools data-ready
20yr
Historical depth advantage

FY28 gate: 20/20 schools on Central Master ID is the technical prerequisite for campaign-scale AI modeling. HLS, GSD, and HGSE migrations are the critical path.

Pillar III
Ethical Guardrails
Responsible AI framework: privacy, transparency, bias auditing

AI in advancement touches sensitive personal and financial data for 400K+ individuals. A responsible AI framework is not optional — it is a donor trust and legal compliance requirement that protects Harvard's reputation and relationship capital.

Data Privacy
FERPA-aligned data handling. PII encryption in transit and at rest. School-level data access controls. No third-party data sharing without written consent.
Stakeholder Transparency
Model cards for every deployed AI tool describing purpose, training data, and limitations. Dean and gift officer briefings before new model rollouts. Schools can see exactly what data they share and receive.
Bias Auditing
Quarterly disparate-impact audits across age, geography, and engagement bands. Human-in-loop requirement for all solicitation decisions at $10M+. SHAP explainability for officer-facing model outputs.

Ethical AI Charter: Harvard's advancement AI program operates under a published charter reviewed annually by the VP Development, General Counsel, and Faculty Advisory Committee. Any model flagged for systematic bias is immediately suspended pending audit.

How the Three Pillars Connect — AI Initiative Gate Process
1
Mission Gate
Define measurable goal. Assign mission owner. Set baseline metric.
→
2
Data Gate
Verify 2yr history. Clean duplicates. Confirm Master ID sync.
→
3
Ethics Gate
Privacy review. Bias pre-audit. Human-loop protocols defined.
→
✓
Deploy
90-day checkpoint. Dashboard monitoring. Quarterly ethics review.
2.2 · School PACER Tree · Cross-School Collaboration

Harvard School Operating Tree – Portfolio-to-Leadership View

This page is the executive school portfolio map. Each school popup now acts as a lightweight Strategic Intelligence Hub: PACER readiness, department/center branches, New Track Alerts, and a Whisper Box intake channel for information from every level of the advancement organization.

Recommended build approach

Use this page as the master school portfolio map first. Then, as each school-level report is created, plug it into the matching school popup rather than crowding the main report page.

Coverage13 + Radcliffeschools / institute map
View ModelPACER + Alertsreadiness · track alerts · whisper intake
Use CaseStrategic Intelligence Hubschool popups become intelligence gateways
Data StatusSyntheticdemo data clearly labeled
Visual Operating Model · School Intelligence Network

Harvard Advancement Intelligence Network

A strategic replacement for a dense matrix: school, department, center, gift-officer, dean, and Whisper Box inputs flow into one leadership-ready intelligence layer without flattening local school autonomy.

New Track Alerts + Whisper Box

Inputs Harvard may not yet see together

The value proposition is not just data collection; it is converting scattered advancement observations into visible leadership signals.

  • Schools: dean priorities, center initiatives, academic themes
  • Gift officers: donor timing, relationship constraints, opportunity shifts
  • Whisper Box: quiet patterns, friction, and internal knowledge
  • External track alerts: market, policy, philanthropic, and global signals
Central layerStrategic Intelligence HubGoverned translation from local signal to leadership action

    For the President

    For Schools

    Local school context stays intact while central standards reduce duplicated work and unclear handoffs.

    Section 3.1 · Data Governance & Ethical AI Readiness

    Data Governance & Ethical AI Readiness

    Technical readiness is not only infrastructure. This section shows the governance, AI ethics, school data readiness, and adoption model required before any school-level report, alert, or dashboard can be trusted by leaders.

    Section 3.1 · visible operating model

    Data Governance & Ethical AI Readiness — info, plan, and samples

    This is the readiness layer that must sit underneath every school-level dashboard, New Track Alert, Whisper Box submission, and VP briefing. It is intentionally visible now: no hidden tabs are required to understand the model.

    Synthetic readiness pulse 78% Ready for controlled school pilots
    01

    Data Foundation

    Info: Consolidates donor, alumni, gift, engagement, school-affiliation, and stewardship records into a clean read layer.

    Plan: Map required fields, deduplicate identities, label systems of record, and flag missing school ownership.

    SampleFAS pilot: 86% complete recordsTop gap: cross-school relationship owner
    02

    Governance & Access

    Info: Defines who can view, edit, export, escalate, or brief each class of advancement intelligence.

    Plan: Create an access matrix for VP, dean, school officer, analyst, stewardship, and events roles.

    Sample42 role / workflow checkpointsAudit trail required for exports
    03

    Ethical AI Review

    Info: Protects trust by requiring source labeling, bias review, privacy checks, explainable scoring, and human approval.

    Plan: Use five gates before AI-generated content enters a leadership dashboard or donor-related workflow.

    Sample5 AI review gatesSource · privacy · bias · accuracy · tone
    04

    Adoption & Measurement

    Info: Confirms whether dashboards and alerts actually change VP, dean, officer, and analyst decisions.

    Plan: Track open rate, action rate, closed-loop follow-up, cycle time, and leader trust feedback.

    Sample73% weekly leader useMeasured across pilot leadership briefings
    30 daysInventory + ownership

    Confirm school data owners, dashboards, field gaps, escalation paths, and privacy boundaries.

    60 daysPilot + review gates

    Run two school pilots using synthetic report samples and human approval checkpoints.

    90 daysScale + train

    Publish school plug-in templates, train contributors, and launch monthly trust/readiness reviews.

    How this connects to the rest of the report: Section 2.2 school trees provide the portfolio view. Section 3.1 makes the data trustworthy. Section 5.4 converts approved signals into leadership-ready decision products.

    Master Architecture – The "One Harvard" Donor Warehouse

    What this diagram shows: Each school has its own CRM. The Central Donor Master is a "read layer" that synchronizes identity, capacity, and engagement state – without taking over or replacing school systems. UD&AS sits in the middle, coordinating across all 12.

    School-Specific Data Connections

    Each School maintains a local CRM and operational data model. The federated Central Donor Master synchronizes a defined slice of constituent state without overriding school-level autonomy. Click each School below to view its connection schema.

    Data Governance & Ethical AI

    Federated Privacy Posture

    • Data minimization: Only the federated slice (Identity, Capacity, Engagement state, Current Owner) crosses School boundaries – full transactional history remains at the School of origin.
    • FERPA & GDPR: Alumni records honor opt-out flags, region-specific deletion rights, and explicit consent for predictive modeling.
    • Ethical AI charter: Propensity models are documented (Model Cards), audited quarterly for disparate impact across age, region, and engagement bands, and require human-in-the-loop review before any 8-figure solicitation.
    • Model transparency: Every constituent can request a plain-language explanation of why they appear on a gift officer's priority list – in compliance with emerging AI transparency requirements.
    Sample executive infographic · syntheticExtension planning detail
    Modeled opportunity$33.1M12-month leadership lens
    Data readiness80%CRM + identity confidence
    Next actions12owner-routed items
    Readiness pathway
    Leadership sample

    Annual fund, lifelong learning, and alumni network activation.

    Automation Samples

    Automation & The Solutions Stack

    Each automated system functions simultaneously as an operational tool for gift officers and as a compliance engine for the Office of the Controller and University Auditor – providing executive oversight, fiduciary integrity, and a defensible audit trail at every stage of the gift lifecycle.

    ⚖️
    Executive Oversight

    Fiduciary Integrity & Compliance: The Automation Layer as Executive Control

    Each automated workflow was designed with two audiences in mind: the gift officer who uses it daily, and the University Auditor and Office of the Controller who rely on its outputs to satisfy regulatory, GAAP, and donor-intent obligations.

    Function 1
    🔒 Compliance Engine

    Every scoring model, wealth reconciliation, and gift classification step produces a structured log entry that is immutable, timestamped, and attributable to a specific system version. This means that if a gift officer's recommendation or a solicitation decision is ever questioned – by legal, by an auditor, or by a dean – the underlying data trail can be reconstructed in full.

    • Model version stamped on every propensity score
    • Ethical AI audit log: every 8-figure solicitation flagged for human review
    • FERPA opt-out flag enforced at query level – not application level
    • Vendor disagreement flags trigger mandatory researcher review queue
    Function 2
    📋 Audit Trail for the Controller

    The Gift Administration Velocity SQL view (Section VII.3) is not only an operational dashboard – it is the primary data feed for the Office of the Controller's gift reconciliation process. Every gift passes through a five-stage lifecycle (Receive → Code → Verify → Acknowledge → Audit) with timestamps at each gate. SLA breach flags serve as automatic escalation triggers, ensuring no gift enters GAAP records without a complete, reviewed trail.

    • GAAP-compliant record created at processing completion
    • 8-figure gifts: 4-hour SLA with same-day presidential acknowledgment
    • SLA breach rate monitored live in VP Husson dashboard
    • Audit complete timestamp feeds directly to Controller reporting
    Function 3
    🏛️ University Auditor Readiness

    The Master ID Matcher (Section VII.4) addresses one of the most persistent risks in university advancement: duplicate constituent records that can lead to mis-attributed gifts, incorrect donor recognition, or undisclosed conflicts of interest. Probabilistic deduplication with documented match thresholds satisfies the University Auditor's requirement for an auditable identity governance record across all 12 school CRMs.

    • Match threshold (Jaro-Winkler ≥0.85) documented and versioned
    • All candidate pairs logged – matched and non-matched – for review
    • Cross-school deduplication reduces gift mis-attribution risk
    • Quarterly data governance review by AVP and General Counsel's Office

    Executive Accountability Map – Automation System → Oversight Body

    Automated SystemPrimary Operational UseCompliance / Governance UseOversight BodyReview Cadence
    Propensity Scoring (VII.1)Weekly gift officer priority listEthical AI audit log; disparate-impact report; Model CardData Governance Committee; Legal CounselQuarterly audit + annual external review
    Wealth Screening Ensemble (VII.2)Trusted capacity estimate for solicitationVendor disagreement queue; capacity ceiling documentationUniversity Research Office; Gift OfficersMonthly vendor reconciliation; annual RFP review
    Gift Administration Velocity (VII.3)Live VP Husson processing dashboardGAAP gift record; SLA breach escalation; Controller feedOffice of the Controller; University AuditorDaily monitoring; monthly Controller reconciliation
    Master ID Matcher (VII.4)Duplicate outreach preventionIdentity governance record; cross-school attribution logUniversity Auditor; General Counsel's OfficeQuarterly governance review; annual full-corpus audit

    Executive Note – From Operations to Governance

    The distinction between "running a good advancement operation" and "demonstrating fiduciary leadership" lies in the documentation layer. Any university can automate a process. The AVP-level contribution is designing automation that simultaneously serves the gift officer, the VP, the Controller, and the Auditor – without adding manual overhead to any of them. This is the governance architecture embedded in the systems below.

    1. Automated Propensity Scoring (Python · XGBoost)

    What This Does – Plain English

    Harvard has 400K+ alumni. It's impossible to manually evaluate who might be ready to give a major gift. This script runs every night at 3am and produces a score (0–1) for each alumnus – like a credit score, but for philanthropic readiness. Monday morning, every gift officer gets a ranked list: "These 50 people are most ready to hear from you this week."

    Input
    Alumni Data
    Giving history, events attended, wealth signals, email opens
    Model
    XGBoost AI
    Trained on 10yrs of Harvard giving outcomes · 84% precision
    Output
    Priority List
    Top 50 "ready now" prospects per gift officer
    Action
    Gift Officer
    Uses list to book calls, visits, President trip stops
    Model Precision & Prospects Surfaced – FY2022 to FY2026
    click to expand ↗

    The Code

    # udas_propensity.py – nightly batch scoring (SYNTHETIC DEMO)
import pandas as pd
import xgboost as xgb
from sqlalchemy import create_engine
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import mlflow

ENG = create_engine(os.environ["UDAS_WAREHOUSE"])

def load_features():
    return pd.read_sql("""
        SELECT c.constituent_id,
               wp.estimated_capacity,    – DonorSearch + iWave ensemble
               wp.wealth_score,          – 1–10 normalized
               g.lifetime_giving,
               g.years_since_first_gift,
               g.gift_count_5yr,
               e.events_attended_3yr,
               e.email_open_rate,
               e.reunion_participation_flag,
               a.school_count,           – multi-school affiliation flag
               a.is_board_member,
               liq.liquidity_events_6mo  – IPO / M&A / vesting signal
        FROM constituent c
        LEFT JOIN wealth_profile wp    ON wp.constituent_id = c.constituent_id
        LEFT JOIN giving_summary g     ON g.constituent_id  = c.constituent_id
        LEFT JOIN engagement_summary e ON e.constituent_id  = c.constituent_id
        LEFT JOIN affiliation_rollup a ON a.constituent_id  = c.constituent_id
        LEFT JOIN liquidity_events liq ON liq.constituent_id = c.constituent_id
        WHERE c.deceased = false AND c.opt_out_modeling = false
    """, ENG)

def score(df):
    with mlflow.start_run(run_name="udas-propensity-v3"):
        model = xgb.XGBClassifier()
        model.load_model("models/propensity_v3.json")
        X = StandardScaler().fit_transform(df.fillna(0).drop(columns=["constituent_id"]))
        df["p_5yr_gift"]   = model.predict_proba(X)[:,1]
        df["p_principal"]  = df["p_5yr_gift"] ** 2.4  # tail-weighted for $5M+ asks
        df["model_version"] = "v3.1.2"
        mlflow.log_metric("rows_scored", len(df))
        mlflow.log_metric("high_propensity_count", (df["p_principal"] > 0.75).sum())
    return df[["constituent_id", "p_5yr_gift", "p_principal", "model_version"]]

if __name__ == "__main__":
    out = score(load_features())
    out.to_sql("propensity_score", ENG, if_exists="replace", index=False)
    print(f"✅ Scored {len(out):,} constituents. High-propensity: {(out.p_principal > 0.75).sum()}")

    2. Wealth Screening Refresh – Ensemble Model

    What This Does – Plain English

    We buy wealth data from three vendors (DonorSearch, iWave, WealthEngine). Sometimes they disagree wildly on a donor's estimated net worth. This script reconciles the three signals into a single trusted number, and flags any case where vendors disagree by more than 30% for a human researcher to review.

    # wealth_ensemble.py – SYNTHETIC DEMO
def blend_capacity(ds: float, iw: float, we: float) -> dict:
    """
    Median-of-three with disagreement alarm.
    If vendors disagree > 30%, flag for manual review queue.
    """
    vals = sorted([v for v in [ds, iw, we] if v is not None])
    if not vals:
        return {"capacity": None, "flag": "NO_DATA"}
    median = vals[len(vals) // 2]
    spread = (max(vals) - min(vals)) / median if median else 0
    flag = "REVIEW_REQUIRED" if spread > 0.30 else "ACCEPTED"
    return {
        "capacity": round(median, -4),  # round to nearest $10K
        "flag": flag,
        "spread_pct": round(spread * 100, 1),
        "sources": {"donor_search": ds, "iwave": iw, "wealth_engine": we}
    }

# Example: $10M (DS), $8M (iWave), $12M (WE) → median $10M, 40% spread → REVIEW
result = blend_capacity(10_000_000, 8_000_000, 12_000_000)
# → {"capacity": 10000000, "flag": "REVIEW_REQUIRED", "spread_pct": 40.0}

    3. Gift Administration Velocity – SQL Dashboard

    What This Does – Plain English

    When a donor sends a $5M check, it must be received, verified, coded, processed, acknowledged, and audited. Our target is under 48 hours for all gifts. This SQL view powers the live VP dashboard and fires an alert automatically if any gift exceeds the SLA window.

    Receive
    Gift Arrives
    Check, wire, stock, or bequest notification
    Code
    Auto-Classify
    AI suggests fund code + school allocation
    Process
    Human Review
    Gift officer confirms within SLA window
    Acknowledge
    Auto Thank-You
    Personalized letter generated same-day
    Audit
    GAAP Record
    Compliance trail auto-logged
    -- velocity_view.sql – powers VP Husson live dashboard (SYNTHETIC DEMO)
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW v_gift_admin_velocity AS
SELECT
  date_trunc('month', accepted_at)                              AS month,
  school_code,
  count(*)                                                       AS gifts_processed,
  round(avg(extract(epoch from finalized_at - accepted_at)
    / 86400.0), 1)                                              AS avg_days,
  percentile_cont(0.95) within group
    (ORDER BY finalized_at - accepted_at)                        AS p95_days,
  sum(CASE WHEN amount >= 1000000  THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)           AS seven_fig_count,
  sum(CASE WHEN amount >= 10000000 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)           AS eight_fig_count,
  -- SLA breach: > 48hrs for standard, > 4hrs for 8-figure gifts
  sum(CASE WHEN
    (amount < 10000000 AND finalized_at - accepted_at > interval '48 hours') OR
    (amount >= 10000000 AND finalized_at - accepted_at > interval '4 hours')
  THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)                                           AS sla_breaches
FROM gift_lifecycle
WHERE finalized_at IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY 1, 2
ORDER BY 1 DESC, avg_days DESC;

    4. Federated Master ID Matcher (Cross-School Deduplication)

    What This Does – Plain English

    Twelve schools each have their own donor records. The same person might appear as "John R. Smith" in one system and "J. Robert Smith" in another. This script links all records for the same person across all 12 CRMs – ensuring no donor is ever double-counted or double-solicited.

    # master_id_matcher.py – Fellegi-Sunter probabilistic match (SYNTHETIC DEMO)
from recordlinkage import Index, Compare, ECMClassifier

def link_school_to_master(school_df, master_df):
    """
    Probabilistic record linkage across 12 School CRMs.
    Uses Jaro-Winkler name similarity + birth year + email domain.
    Generates the lookup_id that anchors every Central Donor Master record.
    """
    indexer = Index()
    indexer.block("last_name")  # block on surname for performance
    candidate_pairs = indexer.index(school_df, master_df)

    cmp = Compare()
    cmp.string("first_name", "first_name",  method="jarowinkler", threshold=0.85)
    cmp.exact( "birth_year", "birth_year")
    cmp.string("email_domain", "email_domain", method="levenshtein", threshold=0.70)
    cmp.geo(   "lat", "lng", "lat", "lng", method="exp", offset=1.0)

    features = cmp.compute(candidate_pairs, school_df, master_df)
    matches  = ECMClassifier().fit_predict(features)
    return matches  # multi-index of matched (school_id, master_id) pairs
    FY2026 Automation Impact: Propensity model (84% precision) surfaced 380 unmanaged $10M+ prospects · $180M+ pipeline unlocked. Gift admin automation reduced processing time from 4.6 days (FY24) to 2.1 days. SLA breach rate: 2.1% (target <3%). Same-day acknowledgment: 96% of 8-figure gifts.
    Section 3.2a · Core AI Workflows

    Core Automation Workflows

    Three automation workflow categories that directly address the largest administrative burdens in advancement — converting time spent on process into time spent on donors. All workflows are designed to augment gift officers, not replace them.

    Strategic Intent

    Automate the predictable. Liberate the irreplaceable.

    Advancement professionals spend an estimated 40–60% of their time on tasks that AI can handle at comparable or superior quality: data lookups, meeting note summaries, draft communications, gift routing, and prospect scoring. Every hour reclaimed is an hour redirected to authentic donor conversation — the only activity that directly drives campaign results.

    40%
    Admin time target savings
    1.8d
    Gift cycle vs. 4.6d FY24
    90%
    Prospect signal precision
    $250M
    High-intent pipeline activated
    Workflow 1
    Donor Intelligence & Segmentation
    Identify high-intent donors before your competitors do
    84%
    Signal precision

    Predictive AI models score every constituent in the Harvard donor database weekly across seven signals — recency, engagement velocity, wealth screening, event attendance, email behavior, liquidity events, and peer network giving. The result is a ranked, actionable list of "high-intent" donors most likely to upgrade, renew, or lapse — delivering it to gift officers before the opportunity window closes.

    Core Capabilities
    ▸Upgrade scoring: identifies donors ready to move from $10K to $100K+ giving levels
    ▸Lapse prediction: 45-day silence trigger alerts with re-engagement recommendation
    ▸Bequest likelihood: Leave a Legacy model with 35:1 ROI for planned giving identification
    ▸Liquidity alerts: IPO, M&A, and vesting event detection via Bloomberg/PitchBook webhook
    Recommended Tools
    DonorSearch AI
    Wealth screening + philanthropic history scoring. Nightly API refresh for real-time capacity updates.
    Dataro
    Donor propensity scoring optimized for nonprofit & education sector. Renewal and upgrade prediction with weekly ranking delivery.
    Harvard XGBoost Model
    In-house gradient boosting model trained on 400K+ constituents, 20-year history. School-specific splits with SHAP explainability.

    Impact target: Activate 124 high-capacity alumni currently without outreach due to manual workflow bandwidth limits. At average $100K gift, this represents $12.4M in latent annual pipeline.

    Workflow 2
    Personalized Engagement at Scale
    Every donor feels like the only donor
    10K+
    Personalized touchpoints/yr

    Generative AI drafts personalized communications — thank-you messages, appeal letters, and annual impact reports — in Harvard's institutional voice but tailored to each donor's known interests, giving history, and school affiliations. Gift officers review and approve, spending 5 minutes on a letter rather than 45. The donor receives something that feels personal; the officer retains full editorial control.

    Communication Types Automated
    ▸Thank-you letters: personalized within 24 hours of gift receipt — matching donor's named interests
    ▸Appeal letters: segmented by school, interest cluster, and giving tier — not mass-broadcast
    ▸Impact reports: annual custom report showing exactly how each major donor's gift performed
    ▸Re-engagement nudges: AI-generated outreach for lapsed donors with tailored re-connection framing
    Recommended Platforms
    Virtuous Momentum
    Provides daily prioritized task lists for gift officers — each morning, the officer sees their top 5 donor touchpoints ranked by impact. Includes draft messages for each interaction.
    Claude (Anthropic) + Custom Prompts
    Generative AI for stewardship content with institution-specific voice guides. Harvard's brand team reviews output templates quarterly.
    Affinaquest (Salesforce) AI Features
    Native CRM automation for gift acknowledgment routing, stewardship timelines, and school-coordination workflows.

    The human-touch principle in action: AI drafts; humans approve and send. The officer's name, signature, and judgment are always on every communication. Donors interact with a person — AI handles the preparation.

    Workflow 3
    Administrative Efficiency
    Eliminate busywork. Protect thinking time.
    -40%
    Grant application time

    The advancement office's most expensive resource is experienced professional time. Every minute spent on data entry, meeting summaries, and grant research is a minute not spent on relationships. Workflow 3 targets the specific administrative "busywork" categories that consume the most staff hours — automating them without sacrificing quality or compliance.

    Busywork Categories Automated
    ▸Data entry: automatic CRM population from emails, call notes, and event check-ins via NLP
    ▸Grant research: AI aggregates foundation profiles, deadlines, alignment scores, and prior award data automatically
    ▸Meeting summaries: automatic post-meeting note generation with donor record update recommendation
    ▸Matching gift processing: automated employer match identification and claim submission — 82%→95% capture rate
    ▸Portfolio hygiene: AI flags stale prospect records, duplicate contacts, and missing data fields for officer review
    Recommended Tools
    Optimized Ask
    Automatically recommends a realistic, data-driven ask amount for each donor based on giving history, peer comparisons, and capacity screening — removing the guesswork and inconsistency from solicitation amounts.
    Automated Intent Classification (AIC)
    UD&AS's in-house NLP system classifies incoming gifts at intake — routing restricted, unrestricted, and exceptional items to the correct processing queue with 94% accuracy.
    eCivis / Instrumentl (Grant Research)
    AI-powered grant discovery and deadline management. Reduces average grant research time from 18 hours to 11 hours per application cycle.

    Cumulative impact: if each of Harvard's 140+ gift officers saves 6 hours per week on administrative tasks, that is 43,680 professional-hours per year redirected to donor relationships — the equivalent of 21 full-time staff added at zero marginal cost.

    Workflow Integration Summary — Deployment Status & Priority

    WorkflowCategoryTool / SystemStatusFY26 TargetKey Metric
    Propensity ScoringDonor IntelligenceHarvard XGBoost + DonorSearchLive90% precision$250M pipeline activated
    Lapse PredictionDonor IntelligenceDataro + HCF RFM ModelLive45-day alert triggerRetention 74.8%→80%
    Thank-you AutomationPersonalized EngagementAffinaquest + AI DraftPilot24-hr turnaround+15% retention
    Daily Task PrioritizationPersonalized EngagementVirtuous MomentumPilot140 officers3× more donor face time
    Automated Ask AmountAdministrative EfficiencyOptimized AskBuildQ3 FY26 deploy+22% solicitation accuracy
    Intent ClassificationAdministrative EfficiencyAIC (in-house NLP)Live1.8-day cycle94% routing accuracy
    Matching Gift CaptureAdministrative EfficiencyHEP Match / AutomationLive95% capture rate+$1.5M/yr matching uplift
    Grant Research AIAdministrative EfficiencyInstrumentl / eCivisBuildQ2 FY26 pilot-40% application time
    Campaign Command Centre

    Dashboards – Multi-Scale Views

    Campaign Command Centre spanning current-year through 20-year horizons. Select a time scale and click any chart to expand with full detail. Dashboards are always-visible; no accordion wrapping.

    Data sources: DonorSearch + iWave ensemble wealth signals, alumni engagement scores (event attendance, email activity, reunion participation), and historical giving trajectories. All figures synthetic for architectural demonstration.
    Healthy operating habit: refresh operational dashboards daily or weekly, and refresh strategic tabs monthly or quarterly. Click for the recommended cadence by tab.

    Donor Interest to Presidential Priority – Decision Map

    This section answers a practical leadership question: which donor interests should be converted into which presidential-priority asks, and who should act next? The score combines interest fit, gift capacity, school readiness, and timing so leadership can prioritize the next conversation.

    How to read this: a high score alone is not enough. The best leadership move is where donor interest, capacity, school readiness, and timing overlap. This turns a heatmap into a call list. Healthy habit: refresh this donor-interest view monthly, and immediately after major liquidity events, new dean priorities, or presidential travel planning.

    Harvard Business School – Pipeline Overview

    HBS holds the highest concentration of $50M+ capacity alumni, driven by executive compensation, private equity, and venture capital wealth. The Board of Dean's Advisors (BDA) serves as a primary cultivation vehicle. Source context: HBS reports approximately 94,000 living alumni globally (HBS Annual Report).

    Harvard Medical School – Pipeline Overview

    HMS is the primary beneficiary of the university's AI Strategic Plan and faces the most acute federal funding headwinds (NIH cuts). Biotech and pharmaceutical wealth among alumni and affiliated faculty creates concentrated major gift capacity. Source context: HMS reports approximately 17,000 MD and PhD alumni (HMS website).

    ▥
    HMS Pipeline Funnel

    Faculty of Arts & Sciences – Pipeline Overview

    FAS is Harvard's largest constituency with 200,000+ alumni. The annual fund breadth (60,000+ donors) combined with 55th reunion upgrade potential makes FAS the backbone of university-wide fundraising. Viewpoint Diversity Professorships are the primary major gift theme in FY2026.

    SEAS – Pipeline Overview

    The School of Engineering & Applied Sciences has the youngest and fastest-growing major gift constituency, driven by tech industry wealth creation. IPO monitoring and startup liquidity event alerts are key prospecting tools. The Allston campus expansion creates significant naming opportunities.

    Program ThemeEst. Pipeline ($M)Top StageKey Ask# ProspectsPriority
    AI / Machine Learning Research$280MCultivationAI Center Naming18Principal
    Allston Campus Building Naming$180MSolicitationBuilding / Lab8Principal
    Climate Tech / Clean Energy$145MFeasibilityResearch Fund24Major
    Quantum Computing$92MCultivationEndowed Lab14Major

    HKS – Pipeline Overview

    Harvard Kennedy School alumni include current and former heads of state, cabinet members, and global policy leaders. International pipeline – particularly Middle East sovereign wealth adjacent donors – is the highest-growth segment. Civil Discourse Initiative is the primary major gift theme.

    Program ThemeEst. Pipeline ($M)Top StageKey Ask# ProspectsPriority
    Civil Discourse Initiative$80MSolicitationEndowed Chair8Principal
    Democracy & Governance Program$120MCultivationNamed Program18Major
    International Development$180MFeasibilityCenter Naming28Major
    Climate Policy$95MCultivationResearch Fund22Major

    HLS – Pipeline Overview

    Harvard Law School's 50,000+ alumni include an exceptional concentration of senior partners at major firms, federal judges, and public figures. Estate/planned giving is the highest per-prospect value segment. Clinical program expansion (Access to Justice) resonates strongly with donor base.

    Program ThemeEst. Pipeline ($M)Top StageKey Ask# ProspectsPriority
    Access to Justice / Clinical Programs$120MCultivationClinical Center24Major
    Constitutional Law / Democracy$95MFeasibilityEndowed Chair14Major
    Planned Gifts / Bequests$280MStewardshipBequest / Trust340Principal
    Ames Courtroom Renovation$45MSolicitationSpace Naming6Major

    GSD – Pipeline Overview

    The Graduate School of Design benefits from strong real estate wealth among alumni. Climate resilience and sustainable design are the highest-resonance gift themes, connecting to both the university's 2026 fossil-fuel goal and broader ESG investment trends among high-net-worth donors.

    Program ThemeEst. Pipeline ($M)Top StageKey Ask# ProspectsPriority
    Gund Hall Renovation / Naming$80MSolicitationBuilding Naming6Principal
    Sustainable Design Research$65MCultivationResearch Center18Major
    Urban Resilience Program$48MFeasibilityNamed Program14Major

    HGSE – Pipeline Overview

    The Graduate School of Education's alumni include superintendents, education policy leaders, and EdTech entrepreneurs. The intersection of technology and equity creates strong resonance with donors at the intersection of finance and impact investing.

    Harvard's Philanthropic Past, Present & Future

    How the current strategic moment fits within Harvard's century-long arc of philanthropy – and why FY2026 is the pivotal foundation year for the next campaign.

    Click for year-by-year breakdown and FY2028 implications
    2013–2018 · Past
    The Harvard Campaign – $9.6 Billion
    Harvard's largest campaign. Set the global record for university fundraising. Established the infrastructure, volunteer leadership, and institutional confidence UD&AS now builds upon.
    2019–2022 · Bridge
    Post-Campaign Normalisation + COVID Resilience
    Allston campus build-out. HSPH pandemic surge funding. Annual giving compressed by travel restrictions. UD&AS began CRM unification and predictive model development.
    2023–2025 · Current Setup
    Garber Era – Presidential Priorities & Quiet Cultivation
    Presidential Priorities Fund launched. Viewpoint Diversity Chairs in quiet phase. HMS AI Strategic Plan activated. Propensity model v3 deployed. CRM integration 75% complete.
    2026 · Now
    Foundation Year – UD&AS Integration Complete
    All 12 school CRMs unified. AI model v3 live. 380 unmanaged $10M+ prospects surfaced. Fossil-fuel-neutral milestone approaching. Green Gifts at $184M YTD.
    2027–2028 · Near Future
    Feasibility → Quiet Phase → Campaign Launch
    Q1 FY27: Feasibility study commissioned. 20 quiet-phase solicitations ($25M+) begin. $1.5B in commitments secured before FY28 public announcement.
    2028–2034 · Future
    Next Comprehensive Campaign – $10B+ Goal
    UD&AS is the operational backbone: real-time pipeline intelligence, AI relationship tools, 12-school coordination, international digital donor journeys.
    2050 · Long-Term
    Fossil-Fuel-Free Campus Goal
    Philanthropic endowments established in FY2025–FY2030 fund the 2040–2050 transition milestones per the Harvard Sustainability Action Plan.

    What the $63M means

    $63M is the modeled near-term flexible-fund shortfall after expected restricted payout and committed Presidential Priorities Fund coverage are applied to FY2026 operating/research-continuity needs. It is a planning number for the proposal, not an audited Harvard budget figure.

    Gap Analysis – Restricted Endowment vs. Operating Need ($B)

    Where fixed endowment payout ends and where the Presidential Priorities Fund must fill in – FY24–FY28 modelled. Illustrative data.

    Roadmap

    Infrastructure Investment & Road to Readiness

    Investment priorities, 12-month campaign preparation timeline, a scored risk register, and the 12-Week AI Adoption Sprint — the hands-on implementation framework for operationalizing UD&AS automation initiatives. Click any row in the tables for full rationale and methodology.

    Implementation Methodology

    The 12-Week AI Adoption Sprint

    A structured, low-risk pathway from AI concept to operational workflow. The sprint framework is designed to build organizational confidence through measurable early wins rather than attempting institution-wide transformation. Each school that completes a sprint becomes an internal champion for the next wave of adopters.

    Phase 1
    Weeks 1–2
    Assessment & Baseline
    What You Do
    1Audit internal "time sinks" — document every recurring task that takes >30 minutes weekly across your team
    2Document baseline metrics: current gift processing time, donor retention rate, grant application hours, officer meeting frequency
    3Identify one low-complexity, high-frequency process for the pilot — typically donor renewal outreach or gift acknowledgment
    4Validate data readiness: confirm 2+ years of transaction history and clean contact records for pilot cohort
    Deliverable
    Time-sink audit report with ranked list of automation candidates and baseline measurement framework signed off by VP.
    Phase 2
    Weeks 3–6
    Pilot Launch
    What You Do
    1Launch one low-complexity pilot — options: automated donor renewals via lapse-prediction, OR AI-assisted grant application drafting
    2Select 3–5 gift officers as pilot participants — choose early adopters, not skeptics, for initial cohort
    3Run weekly retrospectives with pilot team — collect friction points, successes, and time-savings evidence for the scale case
    4Document all friction points via the IDEAS Portal — UD&AS central team provides automated solutions return within 72 hours
    Deliverable
    Pilot results brief measuring time saved, donor response rates, officer satisfaction, and data quality impact vs. baseline.
    Phase 3
    Weeks 7–12
    Scale & Embed
    What You Do
    1Expand successful pilots to remaining gift officers and all applicable workflow categories — using pilot participants as peer trainers
    2Deliver staff training sessions specifically designed to reduce "AI anxiety" — framing: AI as a personal assistant, not a performance monitor
    3Establish ongoing monitoring: 30-day checkpoint dashboards, bi-weekly dean briefings, friction log reviews via IDEAS Portal
    4Launch second sprint cycle — select next automation candidate from the prioritized time-sink list and repeat the process
    Deliverable
    Sprint completion report showing before/after metrics, adoption rates, staff satisfaction scores, and recommendation for Sprint 2 target workflow.

    Addressing "AI Anxiety" — The Critical Adoption Variable

    Staff reluctance is the most common reason AI adoption initiatives fail. The sprint framework addresses this directly: (1) framing — AI as administrative relief, not surveillance or job replacement; (2) peer champions — early adopters become internal advocates; (3) visible wins — officers see measurable time savings within the first 2 weeks; (4) transparency — staff know exactly what data the AI uses and what decisions remain with them. No AI system in UD&AS makes a solicitation decision. All recommendations require human approval.

    Sprint Success Metrics — What to Measure at Each Gate

    MetricBaseline (Before Sprint)Week 6 CheckpointWeek 12 TargetOwner
    Gift processing time4.6 days avg (FY24)≤3.0 days≤1.8 daysGift Admin + UD&AS
    Donor retention rate74.8% (FY25)No decline from baseline+3pp progress toward 80%HCF + School DOs
    Admin hours per officer/week~18hrs documented in time-sink audit≤15hrs (pilot cohort)≤11hrs (-40% target)Gift Officers + VP
    Grant application time~18hrs per application≤14hrs (pilot)≤11hrs (-40% target)Research Team
    AI tool adoption rate74% (FY25 baseline)Pilot cohort 100%90% all eligible officersUD&AS Data & Tech
    Staff AI anxiety scoreSurvey prior to Week 1Improvement in pilot cohortNet positive org-wideHR + UD&AS

    Every investment below is scored by 3-year ROI, operational risk reduction, and campaign-readiness impact. Rows are clickable for full rationale.

    Investment AreaTypeFY26 Cost3-Year ROI

    Q1 FY27 · Jul–Sep 2026
    Feasibility Study Finalisation
    Top 200 transformational prospects interviewed. Capacity ceiling validated. Preliminary $10–12B range published to VP Husson and President Garber.
    Q2 FY27 · Oct–Dec 2026
    Quiet Phase Launch
    Lead-gift solicitation begins ($25M+). Board engagement matrix activated across all 12 schools. Dean's Consensus Tracker goes live.
    Q3 FY27 · Jan–Mar 2027
    Brand & Messaging
    Public case for support drafted. Collateral aligned with Garber-era priorities. President travel programme to top 15 international markets.
    Q4 FY27 · Apr–Jun 2027
    Internal Launch Readiness
    $2.5B+ committed in quiet phase. School Deans aligned via Consensus Tracker. Campaign Council of 250+ volunteers assembled.
    FY2028 · Public Launch
    Comprehensive Campaign Goes Public
    "For Harvard. For the World." – Multi-year, multi-school, $10B+ goal anchored by transformational gifts cultivated through the UD&AS pipeline.

    Section X · Future Vision

    What's Coming Next in Advancement

    Forward-looking capabilities that will define university advancement in the next 5–10 years. Prepared for leadership: no technical background required.

    Executive Brief: Six Practical Advancement Ideas

    This is the plain-English summary for hiring managers: these are not technology for technology's sake. Each idea improves one leadership workflow: find the right prospect, time the ask, brief the officer, steward the donor, grow planned giving, or expand international giving.

    0-6 monthsLiquidity alerts and data governance.
    6-18 monthsOfficer mobile briefings and AI relationship intelligence.
    18-36 monthsPersonalized stewardship, planned giving, and global journeys.
    open project summary →
    🧠
    AI-Powered Relationship Intelligence
    2027–28
    Ready by FY2027
    AI that reads every interaction a donor has had with Harvard – emails, event attendance, faculty conversations – and suggests the exact right moment and message for the next outreach. Think: a GPS for gift officer conversations.
    🌐
    Real-Time Wealth Signal Monitoring
    Live in FY26
    IPO & M&A Alerts
    When a Harvard alumnus's company goes public or gets acquired, our system flags it within 24 hours. Gift officers can reach out at exactly the moment a donor has the most liquidity – often resulting in the largest gifts.
    📱
    Mobile-First Gift Officer Tools
    FY2027
    On-the-Go Intelligence
    A mobile app that gives gift officers their prospect priorities, donor backgrounds, and talking points before they walk into a meeting – like a personal briefing file in their pocket for every conversation.
    🎯
    Hyper-Personalized Stewardship at Scale
    FY2028
    AI-Generated Impact Reports
    Every major donor receives an annual impact report that's unique to them – showing specifically how their gift funded a student, advanced a research breakthrough, or built a facility. Powered by AI, but deeply human in voice.
    🔮
    Planned Giving AI Model
    FY2027
    Bequest & Estate Intelligence
    A predictive model that identifies, years in advance, which donors are most likely to include Harvard in their will. This is the highest-leverage, longest-horizon play in university fundraising – a $10M bequest from a $250K/year donor.
    🌍
    International Donor Digital Journey
    FY2028
    Asia + Middle East First
    A fully digital cultivation and giving platform built for international donors who rarely visit campus – with WeChat integration for China, Arabic-language stewardship for Gulf donors, and localized giving portals in 12 languages.

    § Investment Priorities: FY2027–FY2030
