    The Strategic Recommendation for FY2027

    The single highest-ROI investment Harvard can make right now is hiring 2 dedicated data scientists within UD&AS (estimated cost: $400K/year fully loaded). Based on comparable peer institutions (Yale, Stanford, Columbia), this investment is projected to surface $180–220M in additional pipeline value within 18 months – a 45-55:1 return ratio. The second priority: a mobile gift officer tool (estimated $200K build + $80K/year licensing) that is expected to increase prospect meetings per officer by 30% annually.

    Section XI · Strategic Goal Framework

    S.M.A.R.T.E.R Goals, Logic Models & Probability

    Five strategic goals for FY2026–FY2028, each with a full S.M.A.R.T.E.R breakdown, Logic Model (Inputs → Outcomes → Impact), methodology, and probability-of-success analysis. Designed for VP and leadership review.

    How to Read This Section

    Each S.M.A.R.T.E.R goal maps to one of UD&AS's five strategic objectives for FY2026–FY2028. Each accordion contains: the goal statement, the S.M.A.R.T.E.R breakdown, a Logic Model (Inputs → Activities → Outputs → Outcomes → Impact), methodology, probability of success with supporting drivers, and why it will succeed. These are designed to be read by the VP and leadership team as a decision framework.

    Probability of success comparison for prioritization
    click to expand ↗
    Prioritization read: CRM integration and sustainability are highest-confidence execution moves. Campaign launch and the Presidential Priorities Fund are higher strategic value but carry more external-risk exposure, so they should receive earlier leadership attention and tighter risk review.

    Goal Statement

    Improve propensity model precision from 84% (FY26 baseline) to 90% by end of FY2028, while surfacing 600+ unmanaged $10M+ prospects – increasing principal-gift pipeline value by $250M+ – through weekly AI scoring, school-specific model variants, and real-time liquidity event integration.

    S.M.A.R.T.E.R Breakdown

    LetterThis Goal
    S
    Specific
    		Deploy school-specific XGBoost variants (one per school) using federated training data, real-time IPO/M&A alerts, and weekly refresh cadence – replacing the current single universal model.
    M
    Measurable
    		Precision ≥90% (validated via 18-month holdout). Unmanaged prospects surfaced: ≥600. Pipeline unlocked: ≥$250M. Model drift metric <2% quarter-over-quarter.
    A
    Achievable
    		Stanford, Columbia, and Yale each report 88–92% precision after school-specific model deployment (CASE 2024 benchmarks). Harvard's data depth (20-year history, 400K+ constituents) provides a superior training corpus.
    R
    Relevant
    		Directly supports the $10B+ campaign objective by building the prospect pipeline to the required 3.5:1 coverage ratio before FY2028 launch.
    T
    Time-Bound
    		Phase 1 (school variants): Q3 FY26. Phase 2 (real-time wealth): Q4 FY26. Phase 3 (full precision target): Q4 FY28. Quarterly review against drift metrics.
    E
    Evaluated
    		Monthly: model drift dashboard reviewed by AVP. Quarterly: holdout precision reported to VP Husson. Annual: CASE-benchmarked external review. Mid-cycle corrective action if precision drops >3pp.
    R
    Revised
    		If FY2027 economic conditions compress prospect wealth (market correction scenario), revise unmanaged prospect target to 480 and pipeline target to $180M. Document revision rationale in quarterly board update.

    Logic Model

    Inputs
    3 ML engineers · 20-yr CRM data · DonorSearch + iWave APIs · $1.2M budget
    Activities
    Train school-specific models · Build drift monitoring · Integrate real-time wealth signals
    Outputs
    12 school models · Weekly priority lists · 600 surfaced prospects
    Outcomes
    Gift officers working highest-value prospects · 90% precision · $250M+ pipeline
    Impact
    Campaign-ready 3.5:1 coverage · Transformational gift capacity · One Harvard vision

    Methodology

    Gradient boosting (XGBoost) with school-specific training splits, 5-fold cross-validation, and SHAP explainability for officer-facing summaries. Wealth signals integrated via API (DonorSearch, iWave) on a nightly refresh. Real-time liquidity events (IPO filings, M&A, vesting) via Bloomberg/PitchBook webhook. Ethical AI Charter compliance: quarterly disparate-impact audit across age, region, and engagement bands; human-in-loop for all $10M+ solicitation decisions.

    Probability of Success – FY2028 Target
    82%
    Why it will succeed: (1) Harvard already operates at 84% precision – reaching 90% is an incremental 7pp gain, not a step change. (2) The training data advantage (400K+ constituents, 20-year history) is unmatched among peer institutions. (3) School-specific models are proven at Stanford and Columbia with 25–35% conversion improvement. (4) Three dedicated ML engineers provide sustained iteration capacity that ad-hoc models lack. Primary risk: model drift if economic conditions create distributional shift in wealth signals. Mitigation: drift monitoring with automatic retraining threshold.

    Goal Statement

    Achieve full 12-school CRM integration with real-time bilateral data synchronisation by Q4 FY2026, reducing duplicate prospect outreach by ≥90% and enabling coordinated cross-school major gift asks – projected to increase average coordinated gift size from 2.4× to 3.0× single-school solicitation.

    S.M.A.R.T.E.R Breakdown

    LetterThis Goal
    S
    Specific
    		Migrate GSD, HGSE, and HLS from legacy systems to Affinaquest (Salesforce) with bidirectional Central Donor Master sync. Deploy Dean's Consensus Tracker for prospect arbitration across all 12 schools.
    M
    Measurable
    		20/20 schools in real-time sync. Duplicate outreach incidents: <5/quarter (from current ~18/quarter). Cross-school gift size multiplier: ≥3.0×. Dean's Consensus Tracker adoption: ≥85% of gift officers.
    A
    Achievable
    		9 schools already live. GSD, HGSE, HLS migrations are funded, staffed, and scoped. Duke and Vanderbilt completed comparable Affinaquest rollouts within 6 months. Harvard's procurement is already in contract.
    R
    Relevant
    		The "One Harvard" federated view is a prerequisite for campaign readiness. Every cross-school major gift requires a shared prospect record. The $85M HMS+SEAS AI gift was only possible because both schools shared pipeline data.
    T
    Time-Bound
    		GSD: Q3 FY26. HGSE: Q3 FY26. HLS: Q4 FY26. Dean's Consensus Tracker: Q1 FY27. Full Provost-level dashboard: Q2 FY27.
    E
    Evaluated
    		Monthly: migration status dashboard. Weekly: duplicate-outreach incident log reviewed by Solutions team. Quarterly: VP Husson cross-school coordination report. Post-integration: CASE Advancement Services benchmark.
    R
    Revised
    		If GSD or HLS migration is delayed by legacy data quality issues, isolate the legacy school in a read-only federated view while migration proceeds – ensuring the other 11 schools do not lose coordination capability.

    Logic Model

    Inputs
    $2.8M Affinaquest contract · IT migration team · Data governance charter
    Activities
    Data migration · API integration · Officer training · Consensus Tracker build
    Outputs
    12-school live sync · Duplicate outreach dashboard · Dean's Tracker
    Outcomes
    Zero duplicate outreach · Coordinated cultivation · 3.0× gift multiplier
    Impact
    Campaign operational readiness · Donor trust · $500M+ cross-school pipeline
    Probability of Success – Q4 FY26 Target
    88%
    Why it will succeed: (1) The migration is contractually in progress – not a future decision. (2) 9 of 12 schools are already live, proving the architecture works. (3) The ROI case is irrefutable: the 2.4× cross-school gift multiplier already covers the full $2.8M cost in the first single cross-school gift. Primary risk: legacy data quality at HLS (estate and planned gift records may require manual reconciliation). Mitigation: data quality pre-audit scoped for Q2 FY26.

    Goal Statement

    Commission the campaign feasibility study in Q1 FY2027, secure $1.5B in quiet-phase commitments by Q4 FY2027, and publicly announce a $10B+ comprehensive campaign in FY2028 – making it the largest in Ivy League history, surpassing the original Harvard Campaign ($9.6B).

    S.M.A.R.T.E.R Breakdown

    LetterThis Goal
    S
    Specific
    		Commission Marts & Lundy (or peer firm) feasibility study covering 200 top prospects. Secure minimum 25% of goal ($2.5B) in quiet phase before public announcement. Activate all 12 school campaign sub-goals simultaneously at launch.
    M
    Measurable
    		Feasibility study complete: Q1 FY27. Quiet-phase commitments: $1.5B by Q4 FY27. Campaign goal: $10B+. Annual fundraising target during campaign: $1.6B (FY28), $2.0B (FY29), $2.2B (FY30).
    A
    Achievable
    		Current pipeline coverage: 2.5:1 ($2.8B:$1.1B). By FY28, target 3.5:1. The Harvard Campaign closed at $9.6B against a $6.5B public goal – demonstrating institutional ability to exceed targets. UD&AS data infrastructure is materially more advanced than in 2013.
    R
    Relevant
    		A comprehensive campaign is Harvard's highest-leverage philanthropic tool. It creates urgency, mobilises volunteer leadership, and enables naming opportunities that cannot be made outside a campaign context. The $10B goal aligns with Garber-era AI, sustainability, and financial aid priorities.
    T
    Time-Bound
    		Feasibility study: Q1 FY27. Quiet phase: Q2 FY27 – Q4 FY27. Volunteer leadership assembly: Q3 FY27. Public launch: FY2028. Campaign close target: FY2034 (6-year campaign).
    E
    Evaluated
    		Quarterly: campaign readiness scorecard to Board of Overseers. Annual: CASE peer benchmarking (pipeline depth, volunteer engagement, annual fund participation). Feasibility study provides independent third-party validation.
    R
    Revised
    		If feasibility study reveals principal-gift capacity below $8B, revise campaign goal to $8B (still a record) and extend timeline to 7 years. If economic conditions deteriorate, delay public launch by 12 months while preserving quiet-phase cultivation.

    Logic Model

    Inputs
    $2.8B pipeline · 12 schools · UD&AS AI infrastructure · President + Deans
    Activities
    Feasibility study · Quiet solicitations · Volunteer recruitment · Case-for-support
    Outputs
    $1.5B quiet phase · 250-person council · 12 school sub-goals
    Outcomes
    Public campaign launch · Annual giving ↑ to $1.6B+ · Transformational gifts
    Impact
    $10B+ for AI · aid · sustainability · discourse · health · global access
    Probability of Success – FY2028 Campaign Launch
    76%
    Why it will succeed: (1) Harvard has launched and successfully closed every comprehensive campaign it has ever announced. (2) Current annual-giving trajectory ($1.42B) is already at campaign-year levels. (3) The UD&AS data infrastructure is materially more advanced than in 2013 – enabling faster prospect identification and more precise cultivation timing. (4) Garber-era priorities (AI, sustainability, access, civil discourse) create a compelling multi-theme case for transformational giving. Primary risk: federal funding cuts may absorb donor attention and unrestricted capacity in HMS/HSPH circles. Mitigation: Presidential Priorities Fund surge pre-empts this gap before campaign launch.

    Goal Statement

    Close the remaining $66M gap to the $250M Green Gift target by Q4 FY2026, certifying Harvard's 2026 fossil-fuel-neutral milestone and establishing a permanent sustainability endowment tracking infrastructure for the 2050 net-zero goal.

    S.M.A.R.T.E.R Breakdown

    LetterThis Goal
    S
    Specific
    		Close 8 identified Green Gift prospects (capacity $5M–$22M each) and activate the Salata Institute pipeline for 2 endowed research professorships. Segment donors by operational outcome (electrification, renewables, Allston net-zero, research labs).
    M
    Measurable
    		Total Green Gifts: $250M by June 2026. Endowed sustainability chairs: 12. Donor "climate" flag in CRM: 3,500. Fossil-fuel-neutral certification published by Office for Sustainability: Q4 FY26.
    A
    Achievable
    		74% already achieved ($184M). The remaining $66M requires closing the 8 identified prospects currently in feasibility/solicitation stage. Historical Q4 close rate for sustainability gifts is 42% of annual total – on track.
    R
    Relevant
    		The 2026 milestone is a public commitment made by Harvard's leadership. Missing it damages institutional credibility with climate-focused donors – the fastest-growing philanthropic segment (CASE 2025 benchmarks show 28% YoY growth in sustainability giving).
    T
    Time-Bound
    		Q3 FY26: close 5 of 8 priority prospects. Q4 FY26: close remaining 3. Official certification: June 2026. Post-milestone stewardship report: Q1 FY27.
    E
    Evaluated
    		Monthly: Green Gift dashboard reviewed by AVP and Office for Sustainability. Prospect-level close probability updated in CRM weekly. External milestone validation by Harvard Office for Sustainability (independent of UD&AS).
    R
    Revised
    		If 3 of 8 prospects defer, revise to $230M with a commitment letter bridge covering the gap – enabling certification while full pledges finalise. Communicate transparently with Office for Sustainability and Board.
    Probability of Success – Q4 FY26 Green Gift Target
    86%
    Why it will succeed: (1) Already at 74% with momentum. (2) Only 8 identified prospects needed to close. (3) Q4 is Harvard's strongest giving quarter (33–38% of annual total). (4) Sustainability giving is the fastest-growing philanthropic segment – external tailwind. (5) The milestone is publicly committed – donor urgency is real. Source: Harvard Office for Sustainability, Sustainability Action Plan (public).

    Goal Statement

    Grow the Presidential Priorities Fund from $612M (FY26 YTD) to $900M by FY26 close and $1.5B by FY2028, providing President Garber with a 24-month research-bridge reserve – sufficient to absorb a complete NIH/NSF funding cut for HMS and HSPH without disrupting core research operations.

    S.M.A.R.T.E.R Breakdown

    LetterThis Goal
    S
    Specific
    		Activate the 380 identified "flexibility propensity" donors (historical pattern of general-fund giving). Deploy the "Case for Flexibility" narrative – linking unrestricted gifts directly to the federal funding risk. Assign a dedicated PPF gift officer for prospects with capacity ≥$5M.
    M
    Measurable
    		FY26 close: $900M (68% → 100% of goal). FY28: $1.5B cumulative. Flexibility propensity donors active in cultivation: 380. PPF gifts ≥$1M: 45 closed by FY27. Average PPF gift size: ≥$400K.
    A
    Achievable
    		The $288M Q4 gap requires closing at a $96M/month rate – consistent with Harvard's historical Q4 performance. The 380-prospect cohort has been identified and scored. The federal funding risk narrative is concrete and immediate.
    R
    Relevant
    		Unrestricted capital is the most strategically valuable form of philanthropy Harvard can receive. Without the PPF, a multi-billion-dollar endowment cannot respond to a $180M/year NIH funding gap. This goal directly protects Harvard's research mission.
    T
    Time-Bound
    		$900M: June 30, 2026. $1.0B: Q2 FY27. $1.25B: Q4 FY27. $1.5B: Q4 FY28 (aligned with campaign quiet phase and launch).
    E
    Evaluated
    		Weekly: PPF pipeline reviewed in VP Husson standing meeting. Monthly: progress against flexibility-propensity cohort conversion rate. Quarterly: federal funding risk update to Board. Annual: external benchmarking vs. peer unrestricted fund performance.
    R
    Revised
    		If economic conditions compress mid-level donor capacity, revise FY26 close to $850M and shift FY28 target to $1.35B – maintaining the 24-month bridge reserve logic even with a lower nominal amount.
    Probability of Success – FY26 $900M Goal
    79%
    Why it will succeed: (1) The federal funding risk narrative is real and urgent – donors respond to concrete institutional need. (2) The 380-prospect cohort is identified, scored, and ready for outreach. (3) Q4 is Harvard's highest-velocity giving quarter. (4) Garber administration has publicly prioritised the PPF – lending presidential credibility to the ask. Primary risk: donor fatigue if simultaneously asked for PPF + campaign quiet-phase gift. Mitigation: sequencing model ensures PPF ask precedes any campaign ask by 9–12 months.
    5.2 · Resilience Architecture

    Resilience Architecture: Risk, Trust, and Campaign Readiness

    A redesigned executive control layer for seeing donor fragility, geopolitical exposure, governance maturity, and response readiness. The Donor Fragility Index (DFI) positions UD&AS as an indispensable strategic advisor — able to recommend when each school should pause or accelerate its cultivation activities based on real macro-economic signals.

    Indispensable Strategic Advisor Role

    The Donor Fragility Index: Turning Economic Data Into Cultivation Strategy

    UD&AS is the only office that can synthesize macro-economic signals — equity market volatility, IPO lock-up expiration, real estate cycles, and geopolitical risk — into school-level cultivation recommendations. When the S&P 500 drops 15%, UD&AS advises HBS to pause its $50M+ solicitations while accelerating planned-giving conversations. When a tech IPO matures, it activates the SEAS pipeline. This advisory function, built on the Donor Fragility Index, makes UD&AS irreplaceable to every Dean — regardless of formal authority.

    Pause
    High fragility signal
    Accelerate
    Liquidity event signal
    87%
    Operating resilience
    142
    Relationships monitored
    Leadership Dashboards · Synthetic Example

    Risk intelligence translated into executive actions

    Modeled dashboard cards show how the Donor Fragility Index can help leaders decide when to pause, accelerate, steward, or escalate advancement activity.

    Monitored relationships142principal + major gift prospects
    Pause recommendations18market or sentiment fragility
    Accelerate windows27liquidity/event timing signals
    Readiness score87%governance + response maturity
    Donor Fragility Command View
    HBSPause 3 asks$74.5M exposure
    SEASAccelerateIPO window active
    HMSSteward firstclinical-program sensitivity
    School Advisory Queue
    1
    FAS / HKS joint briefPrepare dean-facing cultivation memo before next event cycle.
    2
    SEAS liquidity signalRoute to principal gifts team within 24 hours.
    3
    HLS planned-giving pivotSwitch from immediate ask to estate-planning conversation.
    Executive Decision Path
    ✓Signal detected
    ✓School context reviewed
    3Leadership action drafted
    4Human approval + outreach

    All figures are synthetic Harvard-style examples for portfolio demonstration only.

    🔴 DFI Pause Signals — Advise Schools to Hold
    Market correction >10%: delay $25M+ solicitations; shift to cultivation and stewardship
    Donor news cycle (negative): pause formal ask; maintain relationship touchpoints only
    Equity lock-up pending: hold timing until post-lock expiry; pre-position the ask
    Geopolitical volatility: defer international donor meetings; substitute with digital stewardship
    School sentiment score <60: pause cross-school coordination; focus on bilateral trust-building
    🟢 DFI Accelerate Signals — Advise Schools to Move
    IPO/M&A liquidity event: activate within 24 hours; pipeline officer contacts prospect same week
    Compensation vest cycle: deploy 90-day ask countdown when RSU/option vest is confirmed
    Portfolio recovery (>12% YTD): re-activate paused $25M+ conversations; lead with impact framing
    Dean alignment confirmed: unlock cross-school joint solicitation; schedule President engagement
    Matching gift employer campaign active: accelerate renewal timing to capture 2× gift opportunity
    Executive signal

    Harvard can protect relationship capital by watching risk before it becomes a donor, school, or campaign issue.

    This section turns resilience into management rhythm: what is stable, what needs a watchlist, who owns the response, and how quickly the organization can act.

    87%Operating resilience index Watchlist-led, owner-visible, campaign-ready
    142Top relationships monitored
    23Watchlist records
    7Critical-touch prospects

    Stable

    Recent engagement, clear ownership, current capacity record, and no conflicting ask.

    Next action: maintain cadence

    Watch

    Engagement slowdown, capacity uncertainty, or cross-school overlap requiring coordination.

    Next action: assign review owner

    At Risk

    Signal conflict, delayed stewardship, news exposure, or unresolved dean/officer alignment.

    Next action: VP escalation queue
    SignalMarket, policy, reputation, or geopolitical trigger
    ValidateResearch confirms relevance and source strength
    RouteOwner receives action with timing window
    BriefVP/dean/president receives concise decision note

    What improves

    Leadership stops relying on anecdotal updates and sees risk by relationship, school, and campaign theme.

    What is protected

    Donor trust, school autonomy, gift timing, stewardship obligations, and presidential travel priorities.

    UD&ASData quality, model audit, dashboard cadence
    Gift OfficersRelationship context, next-best action, donor feedback
    SchoolsDean priorities, local strategy, shared ownership
    LeadershipEscalation, presidential priority alignment, resource decisions
    Governance rule: a risk signal is not a decision. It becomes useful only when it has an owner, a response window, and a documented next action.
    Team GrowthTrack adoption, portfolio hygiene, response velocity, and training needs.
    PerformanceCompare movement, stewardship cycle time, proposal readiness, and regional opportunity.
    EffectivenessMeasure whether dashboard signals convert into meetings, asks, gifts, and resolved conflicts.
    Campaign ReadinessShow which schools, systems, and donor cohorts are ready for a $10B+ campaign arc.

    Operational Blueprint Aligned to the Strategic Intelligence Console

    This portfolio now follows the same topic spine as the strategic intelligence report. The operational sections answer the practical question: once the long-range risk is visible, what should UD&AS build, govern, monitor, and prove at the school level?

AI transitionHuman-AI responsibility, AI governance, staff reskilling, and gift admin modernization. AI signalsPrivacy law, AI risk frameworks, vendor changes, model governance, peer adoption.

Campaign architectureFY26-FY75 roadmap, planned giving, sustainable model, and proposal next step. Opportunity signalsResearch breakthroughs, peer campaigns, donor reputation, public crisis needs, proposal timing.

    System use

    Use the strategic intelligence report to establish strategic urgency, this portfolio to show operational feasibility, and the news-alerts report to keep project and campaign decisions current as external conditions move.

    Section 5.4 · Signature Operating Model

    The Signal Tower Model · Driving Harvard’s Fundraising Future

    This section expands the opening pitch into an operating model: every report, alert, and dashboard becomes a decision signal that leaders can scan, trust, and act on quickly.

    Plain-English purpose Turn complex work into leadership-ready signals.

    Structure first. Evidence second. Design supports comprehension. AI accelerates drafts but never replaces review.

    01ClarifyWhat decision is this report supporting?
    02StructureExecutive summary → evidence → action → appendix.
    03VisualizeUse dashboards, matrices, trees, and cards for scanning.
    04GovernLabel synthetic data, check claims, protect sensitive content.
    05ReleaseContrast, links, popups, mobile, print, and accessibility pass.
    What this replaces

    Not “pretty slides.” A repeatable report operating system.

    The redesign service should not be framed as decoration. It is a production discipline for making advancement intelligence readable, governed, and usable by busy leaders.

    Raw notes→Structured evidence→Executive dashboard→Decision queue
    Reader experience

    Help leaders scan first, then go deep.

    • One message per section.
    • Numbers and risks visible at a glance.
    • Details hidden in popups or appendices.
    • No low-contrast text or off-color textboxes.
    Governance experience

    Make trust visible.

    • Synthetic data is labeled.
    • Claims are source-checked.
    • AI summaries have human review gates.
    • Confidential data is never used casually.
    Fundraising train-control metaphor · decision delivery spineUse the train-control metaphor to show how UD&AS becomes the reliable organizational spine, while VP leadership monitors signals and schools receive dean-ready action.
    Visual metaphor supplied by user. The report system should feel like a Harvard advancement signal tower and rail spine: clear route, clear signal, clear owner, clear destination.
    Metaphor applied: UD&AS is the organizational spine; gift officers are the operational cars carrying relationship intelligence; deans and schools are partnership cars and destination stations; the VP signal tower monitors portfolio progress, funding strategy, risk, and campaign route decisions.
    Green · Proceed to dean-ready brief

    Use when data is labeled, recommendation is clear, and the school owner is confirmed.

    Publish / go
    Yellow · Hold for human review

    Use when the idea is promising but needs source checks, tone review, or privacy review.

    Review / slow
    Red · Stop for risk protection

    Use when confidential data, unsupported claims, low contrast, or broken interactions could reduce trust.

    Stop / protect
    Blue · Route to owner

    Convert New Track Alerts and Whisper Box inputs into owner, confidence, action window, and follow-up slot.

    Assign / close loop
    ProceedDashboard-ready, dean-ready, action-ready.
    HoldNeeds human review or stronger evidence.
    StopRisk, privacy, quality, or trust issue.
    RouteAssign to VP, dean, school officer, analyst, or stewardship.
    New Track Alerts

    External watch layer

    Scans for events that affect project timing, campaign opportunity, donor risk, policy context, reputation, market movement, alumni/current-student news, and research visibility.

    Whisper Box

    Internal listening layer

    Captures frontline observations from everyone: gift officers, coordinators, analysts, events teams, school partners, assistants, stewardship, and data operations.

    Why it matters

    Signals become action

    The system turns loose inputs into a governed queue: classify, confidence-score, assign owner, escalate if needed, and reflect back to the contributor.

    Minimum release checklist Use this before any executive report is shared.
    Bottom line: Section 5.4 is the production standard for the whole suite. It explains how the master plan, school reports, alert reports, and dashboards stay readable, trustworthy, and leadership-ready as the system grows.

    Synthetic Disclaimer & References

    Disclaimer: All donor records, figures, scoring metrics, and architectural references in this portfolio are synthetic and intended for architectural and strategic demonstration only. Public references are limited to:

    • The Harvard Campaign 2013–2018 ($9.6B) – Harvard Gazette & HAA reporting
    • Harvard Sustainability Action Plan – fossil-fuel-free 2050 commitment, fossil-fuel-neutral 2026 milestone (Harvard Office for Sustainability)
    • Presidential Priorities Fund – Garber administration communications (2024–2026)
    • HMS AI Strategic Plan 2025 – public framework (HMS Communications)
    • FY24 Endowment ~$53.2B – Harvard Financial Report FY2024
    • Viewpoint Diversity Initiative – President Garber communications FY2025
    • Uploaded File Redesign Service Options framework – document redesign, AI governance, risk management, and accessibility integration

    Sustainability Scorecard – Markdown Template

    Security note: use /dashboards/sustainability.md only for public, synthetic, or already-approved metrics. For real donor, prospect, gift, or school pipeline data, keep tracking in a permissioned internal system such as Salesforce/Affinaquest, Tableau or Power BI with SSO, SharePoint or OneDrive with role-based access, or a private, access-controlled repository with branch protection and audit logging.

    # 🌱 Harvard Sustainability Scorecard – FY2026
*Tracking the fossil-fuel-neutral milestone | Office for Sustainability × UD&AS*

## Executive Snapshot
| Metric                              | FY24  | FY25  | FY26 YTD | 2026 Goal | Status |
|-------------------------------------|-------|-------|----------|-----------|--------|
| Green Gifts ($M)                    | 118   | 151   | 184      | 250       | ▲ 74%  |
| Endowed Sustainability Chairs       | 3     | 5     | 8        | 12        | 67%    |
| Salata Institute Funding ($M)       | 42    | 58    | 71       | 90        | 79%    |
| Donors flagged "climate" interest   | 2,140 | 2,610 | 2,980    | 3,500     | 85%    |

## Strategic Pillars
1. **Decarbonization Capital** – gifts earmarked for fossil-fuel-free retrofit
2. **Salata Institute Pipeline** – 9-figure asks tied to climate research
3. **Designed Sustainability** – GSD lab + cross-school fellowships
4. **Donor Engagement** – Climate-interest segmentation in Central Donor Master

*Last refreshed nightly via udas_propensity.py*

    Candidate / External Impact Samples

    🌍 UNICEF – Impact Story Report 👤 Candidate Portfolio
    Closing note

    Congratulations on reaching the end of this report. I hope the journey through this plan feels energizing, practical, and imaginative — a view of how AI fundraising technology can give leaders, schools, and gift officers more time, clearer signals, and stronger confidence in the work ahead.

    My hope is that this proposal resonates with Harvard’s needs and opens a conversation about what becomes possible when advancement intelligence is governed well, guided by human judgment, and designed around mission. If we let this AI engine help carry the routine weight, our people can focus more fully on relationships, ideas, discoveries, students, communities, and the work that shapes the world’s future.

    Respectfully,
    Narissara Khomkhai
